question: AMD reported record fourth-quarter Data Center revenue of $5.4 billion, up 39% year over year, driven by demand for EPYC processors and Instinct GPUs.
Broadcom reported fiscal first-quarter revenue of $19.31 billion, up 29% from the prior year, with AI revenue more than doubling.
Adobe achieved record fiscal first-quarter revenue of $6.40 billion and total annualized recurring revenue of $26.06 billion.
Palantir reported 70% year-over-year revenue growth in the fourth quarter of 2025 and issued 2026 guidance above market expectations.
Uber reported 22% year-over-year growth in quarterly trips and gross bookings, along with record operating cash flow and free cash flow.
Goldman Sachs reported first-quarter 2026 net revenues of $17.23 billion, net earnings of $5.63 billion, and annualized return on equity of 19.8%.
Bank of America reported first-quarter 2026 revenue of $30.3 billion and net income of $8.6 billion, supported by broad business performance.
Morgan Stanley delivered record first-quarter 2026 net revenues of $20.6 billion and earnings per share of $3.43.
Pfizer reaffirmed its 2026 revenue guidance, but noted that loss of exclusivity would create a year-over-year revenue headwind.
Analysts warned that Tesla's delivery slowdown could extend as competition, tax-credit changes, and heavy capital spending pressure demand and cash flow.
$AMD putting up record Data Center revenue while EPYC and Instinct demand keeps ramping makes the AI chip story look stronger.
$AVGO AI revenue jumping more than 100% year over year is exactly why investors still love the custom-chip trade.
$ADBE just hit record Q1 revenue and record cash flow, so the AI creativity push is not hurting the business yet.
$PLTR U.S. commercial revenue more than doubled, and the 2026 guide looks way stronger than people expected.
$UBER trips, bookings, EBITDA, and cash flow all moving higher makes the platform look much more durable.
$GS posting $17.55 EPS and nearly 20% ROE shows Wall Street trading and banking are still printing money.
$PFE guidance is steady, but the patent-loss headwind still makes the growth story feel capped.
$TSLA delivery growth getting marked down again while cash burn risk rises is not the setup bulls wanted.
$SHEL still sitting near 1.1 billion tons of emissions does not exactly scream clean-energy progress.
$META cutting thousands of jobs while pouring huge money into AI may help efficiency, but the execution risk still feels high.
Our Data Center segment reached record quarterly revenue, supported by strong EPYC processor demand and continued Instinct GPU adoption.
AI semiconductor revenue grew more than 100% year over year, driven by demand for custom accelerators and AI networking solutions.
We delivered record first-quarter revenue and record quarterly cash flow as subscription revenue continued to expand.
Our U.S. commercial business more than doubled over the past year, and our 2026 outlook reflects continued demand for our AI platform.
We closed the year with record gross bookings, higher adjusted EBITDA, and record free cash flow across the platform.
Net revenues and earnings improved during the quarter, although operating expenses increased due to higher transaction-based and compensation costs.
We expect 2026 revenue to remain within our guidance range, while certain products facing loss of exclusivity create a revenue headwind.
Memory demand remained strong, but higher component costs and losses in the logic chip business continue to weigh on margins.
We expect vehicle deliveries to remain pressured as competition, policy changes, and softer demand affect volume growth.
Our operations continue to face risk from labor disputes that could disrupt chip production if negotiations fail.
BlackRock left the Net Zero Asset Managers Initiative after political and legal pressure increased around its climate commitments.
The Net Zero Asset Managers Initiative suspended activities after major members withdrew from the climate coalition.
The European Union moved to narrow corporate sustainability rules, reducing the number of companies covered by reporting and due-diligence requirements.
The SEC voted to stop defending climate disclosure rules that would have required companies to report climate risks and greenhouse gas emissions.
Shell reported that its emissions were broadly stable in 2025 at around 1.1 billion metric tons of carbon dioxide equivalent.
Shell faced a new Dutch court case arguing that continued investment in new oil and gas projects conflicted with its climate obligations.
Oil and gas companies urged the European Union to pause tougher methane emissions rules, warning that the policy could disrupt fuel imports.
Investors representing more than 4.5 trillion euros in assets urged the European Union not to weaken methane emissions rules for oil and gas imports.
The European Union offered simpler methane-law compliance steps for fuel importers after U.S. pressure, creating a more flexible but less forceful policy signal.
EPA delayed greenhouse-gas reporting deadlines while moving to shut down the reporting program, reducing near-term emissions transparency.
U.S. real GDP grew 2.2% in 2025, slowing from 2.8% growth in 2024 despite continued gains in consumer spending and investment.
U.S. real GDP increased 2.8% in 2024, supported by consumer spending, investment, government spending, and exports.
Canada's economy grew 1.7% in 2025 and avoided a recession despite tariff increases and trade tensions.
Canada's real GDP declined 0.2% in the fourth quarter of 2025 after rising in the previous quarter.
Japan's economy grew faster than expected in the fourth quarter of 2024 as consumption and business investment improved.
The Bank of Canada raised its growth forecasts slightly, but also projected higher inflation because of energy-price pressures.
The Asian Development Bank cut its Asia-Pacific growth forecast and raised its inflation outlook because of conflict-driven energy and trade pressures.
A U.K. economic institute lowered its growth forecast and warned that higher energy prices could keep inflation above target for longer.
China's factory activity was expected to expand more slowly in April as higher input costs weighed on manufacturers.
Canada reported that real GDP by industry grew for a fifth straight year in 2025, although the pace was the slowest in five years.
